title: Te Whakamahi Pāngarau ki te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku | Mathematics of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Mathematics
culturalContext: Students will investigate the health and flow patterns of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, the stream that gives our college its name and flows through Ngāti Kahungunu rohe. Drawing on local pūrākau about the stream's significance as a source of life and navigation, students will apply mathematical reasoning to support the ongoing kaitiakitanga of this taonga.
learningObjectives: Calculate and analyze flow rates and water quality data from Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku using algebraic formulas and statistical measures
Create scale maps and cross-sectional diagrams of the stream using geometric principles and coordinate geometry
Develop mathematical models to predict seasonal changes in stream flow and identify potential environmental impacts
Present findings using appropriate mathematical language and visual representations that honor the cultural significance of the awa

activities.title: He Hīkoi Awa | Stream Walk and Data Collection
activities.description: Students visit three designated points along Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku to measure width, depth, and flow rate using trundle wheels, measuring tapes, and float methods. They record water temperature and clarity, connecting observations to the pūrākau of the stream shared by a local kaumātua.

activities.title: Te Tātari Raraunga | Data Analysis with Statistical Measures
activities.description: Using spreadsheets, students calculate mean, median, and range for their collected data sets. They create scatter plots to identify correlations between variables (e.g., flow rate and water clarity) and discuss what these patterns might indicate about the health of the awa.

activities.title: Te Hoahoa Āwhata | Scale Mapping and Geometric Modeling
activities.description: Students work in groups to create scale drawings (e.g., 1:100) of the stream's cross-sections at each measurement point, applying knowledge of ratios, proportions, and area calculations. They use coordinate grids to plot the stream's path and calculate approximate length using distance formulas.

activities.title: Te Whakamōhiotanga Pāngarau | Mathematical Reporting for the Community
activities.description: Groups synthesize their data, maps, and models into a brief report or digital presentation. They propose one evidence-based recommendation for supporting the stream's health, using mathematical reasoning to justify their idea and connecting it to the concept of kaitiakitanga.

resources: Local kaumātua from Ngāti Kahungunu to share pūrākau and mātauranga about Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Hawke's Bay Regional Council's environmental data portal for historical stream flow information
Trundle wheels, measuring tapes, stopwatches, and water testing kits for field data collection
NZ Maths Digital Learning Object 'Measuring Our Streams' for interactive data analysis techniques

tasks: Completed field data collection sheet with accurate measurements
A4 summary sheet including calculated statistical measures and one scatter plot with a line of best fit
Scale drawing of one stream cross-section with calculations shown
Two-minute group presentation of one key finding and a kaitiakitanga recommendation

nzcAlignment: Mathematics and Statistics Level 5: Statistical investigation; Number strategies and knowledge; Geometric representations
NZC Key Competencies: Using language, symbols, and texts; Participating and contributing
NZC Principles: Cultural diversity; Community engagement; Future focus
NZC Values: Innovation, inquiry, and curiosity; Ecological sustainability; Community and participation
